records.ety: [L. consistere to stand still or firm; con- + sistere to stand, cause to stand, stare to stand: cf. F. consister. See Stand.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Consisted; p. pr. & vb. n. Consisting.]
usage: The verb consist is employed chiefly for two purposes, which are marked and distinguished by the prepositions used. When we wish to indicate the parts which unite to compose a thing, we use of; as when we say, “Macaulay's Miscellanies consist chiefly of articles which were first published in the Edinburgh Review.” When we wish to indicate the true nature of a thing, or that on which it depends, we use in; as, “There are some artists whose skill consists in a certain manner which they have affected.” “Our safety consists in a strict adherence to duty.”

defs: To stand firm; to be in a fixed or permanent state, as a body composed of parts in union or connection; to hold together; to be; to exist; to subsist; to be supported and maintained.
quotes: He is before all things, and by him all things consist.

defs: To be composed or made up; -- followed by of.
quotes: The land would consist of plains and valleys.

defs: To have as its substance or character, or as its foundation; to be; -- followed by in.
quotes: If their purgation did consist in words.
A man's life consisteth not in the abundance of the things which he possesseth.

defs: To be consistent or harmonious; to be in accordance; -- formerly used absolutely, now followed by with.
quotes: For orders and degrees Jar not with liberty, but well consist.
